text: This is a case of a patient who was having pain for two days and came to the hospital where a CT scan was performed and showed an acute perforation of an ulcer. So we brought her emergently to the OR. Luckily, where I practice, I'm able to utilize the robot at all hours of the day. So even though this was a midnight case, I was able to successfully treat her.
Upon entering the abdominal cavity, bile is visibly flowing, with pus and abscesses forming, indicating a perforated ulcer.

The surgeon elevates the liver to inspect the stomach for any gastric perforation, finding it intact. The gallbladder is lifted, revealing it was covering a large ulcer on the lateral wall of the duodenum, measuring approximately two to three centimeters, with visible food remnants lodged in the ulcer.
The surgeon evaluates the duodenal ulcer and decides on a primary closure technique. A 2-0 V-Loc suture is introduced to run the closure of the ulcer.
The surgeon sutures the duodenal wall on the lateral aspect, ensuring the closure comes together smoothly with minimal tension, avoiding narrowing or damage to underlying structures.
The surgeon uses scissors to divide adhesions and free a vascularized tongue of omentum, which is laid over the ulcer site. Interrupted silk sutures are used to secure the omental patch in place.
The final view shows the omental patch securely placed over the closure without tension. There is no visible bleeding or leakage from the surgical site.
